lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms operating bars, taverns, pubs, nightclubs, and other drinking places whose primary business is preparing and serving alcoholic beverages for immediate on-premises consumption as a repeat hospitality service to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Food-primary restaurants, member-only civic or social clubs, packaged-alcohol retailers, dance clubs that do not sell alcohol, beverage manufacturers, shells, captive internal units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Walk-in and repeat consumers generally pay posted menu prices for beverages, tabs, cover charges, and occasional event minimums; revenue is predominantly transactional rather than governed by long-term customer contracts.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and mean wages by occupation inside NAICS 722400, not AI-exposed task hours. I wage-weight the listed occupation groups and apply bounded task-share judgments: higher exposure for management, marketing, bookkeeping, scheduling, inventory, ordering, and payment work; lower exposure for bartending, cooking, security, cleaning, and live hospitality. This can overstate substitution because an exposed task may be inseparable from customer-facing presence, while occupation averages can understate owner-manager administrative work not carried as payroll employment.
a.rationale: BLS reports that food preparation and serving occupations are 83.42% of industry employment, including bartenders at 44.21%, waiters and waitresses at 12.24%, and cooks at 11.90%. Those jobs contain some automatable ordering, payment, training, forecasting, and prep-planning tasks, but their core beverage production, service, monitoring, and hospitality are physical and social. The smaller, more highly paid management and administrative groups contribute disproportionately to wage-weighted exposure.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time-and-wage study of bartenders, shift supervisors, food-service managers, and owner-managers in independent bars, distinguishing already automated work from current manual work, would materially change this range.
caveats: The BLS occupation table is from May 2023 and covers payroll employment rather than unpaid owner labor.
Task shares are judgmental and mean wages are used where detailed wage and employment cells are suppressed.
Restaurant technology surveys cover the broader restaurant population rather than drinking places specifically.

rho.bridge: National Restaurant Association surveys measure technology and AI adoption by restaurants, not the five-year implementation share of exposed work at lower-middle-market bars. I use current adoption as a floor signal and the scarcity of observed job elimination as a constraint on rapid substitution, then allow five years for diffusion into scheduling, administration, marketing, inventory, ordering, and payment. Broader restaurants may have more capital and repeatable workflows than independent bars, biasing implementation upward; bar operators may have unusually strong labor-shortage incentives, biasing it downward.
rho.rationale: In the 2026 survey, 26% of restaurants used an AI-enabled tool, while 49% used scheduling software and only 6% said technology investment during the prior two or three years permanently eliminated jobs. In 2024, 47% expected labor-shortage automation to become more common, but 69% expected technology to augment rather than replace labor. These observations support meaningful adoption of support workflows without assuming most exposed work becomes operationally implemented.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study from major bar POS and workforce-management vendors showing customer adoption, active-feature usage, labor hours per transaction, implementation failures, and avoided hires by venue type would materially move rho.
caveats: Tool adoption does not measure the fraction of exposed task hours actually removed or avoided.
Surveyed restaurants include full-service and limited-service formats and are not restricted to the assigned EBITDA band.
Liquor-control, service-quality, staff acceptance, integration, and data-quality constraints vary by state and concept.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already a coherent external-customer activity: bars, taverns, nightclubs, and drinking places preparing and serving alcohol for immediate consumption. Because the supplied firm count is already limited to the assigned EBITDA band, most firms fit the repeat-service lens. The deduction allows for miscoded food-primary venues, economics inseparable from a celebrity or owner persona, captive venues, and firms whose operations cannot be separated from non-qualifying activities.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census or commercial-establishment file linking NAICS, revenue mix, normalized EBITDA, ownership, captive status, and concept dependence for every candidate would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source directly measures lens eligibility among firms in the supplied EBITDA band.
NAICS is assigned at the establishment level, while the supplied count and this primitive concern firms.
This estimate does not discount for transfer probability, which is handled only in s5.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell observes reported restaurant sales across all small-business sizes, and the National Restaurant Association reports broad restaurant-owner demographics; neither measures five-year control transfers among eligible lower-middle-market bars. I treat 2,516 reported restaurant sales in 2025 as evidence of an active transfer channel and combine it with the owner-age signal to bound a five-year control-transfer probability. Marketplace undercoverage of private deals biases the signal low, while the much smaller median sold business and the broader restaurant population may transact more often than lower-middle-market bars, biasing it high; the net direction is ambiguous.
s5.rationale: BizBuySell reported 2,516 restaurant transactions in 2025, with median cash flow of $115,252, which demonstrates liquidity but is far below the assigned firm band. Restaurant-owner data show 29% of owners were under age 45, offering only a broad succession signal. A moderate probability allows for retirements, strategic sales, and distress exits while excluding closures, asset-only liquidations, minority investments, and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and broker transaction records matched to the supplied firm universe, with deal type, EBITDA, seller age, asset sale versus going-concern status, and control outcome, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Reported marketplace sales are not a complete census and are concentrated below the assigned EBITDA band.
Restaurant transactions are not bar-specific, and owner age does not itself imply sale intent.
Liquor-license and lease transfer rules can convert an intended sale into an asset liquidation or failed closing.

q.rationale: Customer pricing is predominantly posted and transactional, so there is no automatic contractual pass-through of labor savings and an operator can initially retain a meaningful share. Retention erodes through local price competition, promotions, service reinvestment, wage increases for remaining staff, and renewal-like customer choice on every visit. The 2024 survey found 30% of restaurant operators planned flexible or dynamic menu-pricing investment, while 2025 sold-restaurant data showed revenue rising much faster than cash flow, evidence that nominal gains do not automatically persist as margin.
q.change_evidence: Venue-level panel data linking implemented automation to gross labor savings, menu prices, discounts, tips, traffic, service scores, and EBITDA for five years, with matched non-adopters in the same local markets, would directly estimate retained benefit.
caveats: No observed study isolates AI-derived gross benefit and its pass-through in bars.
Competitive intensity, concept differentiation, tipping practices, and local minimum wages create wide dispersion.
The estimate excludes implementation failure and volume loss, which belong in rho and demand primitives.

d5.bridge: BLS projects drinking-place employment, not constant-price constant-quality service quantity, and publishes real output only for the broader food-services-and-drinking-places sector. I convert the drinking-place employment compound annual growth rate of 0.4% from 2024 to 2034 into a five-year ratio near 1.02 and use the broader sector's 2.2% real-output growth rate as an upper anchor near 1.11. Employment can understate quantity if productivity rises; broader-sector output can overstate bar demand because food-service formats may grow faster.
d5.rationale: BLS projects drinking-place employment rising from 421,200 in 2024 to 439,900 in 2034, while broader sector real output rises at 2.2% annually. Against that, NIAAA reports per-capita ethanol consumption fell 1.2% in 2023 as beer and wine declines outweighed spirits growth. The base therefore assumes approximately flat per-capita underlying demand with modest aggregate growth, while the range allows health-driven moderation or stronger experiential spending.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts and historical panels of inflation-adjusted on-premises bar sales, visits, drinks served, closures, and openings from Circana, Technomic, NielsenIQ, state alcohol-tax agencies, and Census would materially change d5.
caveats: Employment is affected by productivity and staffing intensity and is not a direct quantity measure.
Broader sector output includes restaurants, and total alcohol consumption includes off-premises channels.
Pandemic recovery, demographic shifts, cannabis substitution, wellness trends, and local regulation add structural uncertainty.

o.rationale: The current service basket is primarily an on-premises social experience combining beverage preparation, hospitality, age and conduct monitoring, entertainment, and a regulated retail location. Ordering and payment can become self-service, but software does not supply the venue or assume accountable alcohol-retailer duties. Restaurant research likewise finds employee engagement integral to full-service experiences even while many consumers welcome digital ordering and payment.
o.change_evidence: Observed market-share and visit-frequency data for staff-light bars, automated beverage walls, self-pour venues, virtual nightlife substitutes, and home-consumption alternatives, combined with state regulator treatment of accountable operators, would materially change o.
caveats: Full-service restaurant preferences are a proxy for bar patrons and may overstate food-service-style employee interaction.
State and local licensing requirements vary, and regulation can change over the horizon.
This primitive concerns operator requirement, not labor intensity; staff reductions are captured in a and rho.

narrative.executive_view: Drinking places offer a real but bounded opportunity to automate support workflows around scheduling, marketing, inventory, hiring, ordering, payment, and compliance. The central service remains a physical, social, regulated experience, so the operating case should rest on consistent execution and avoided hiring rather than wholesale replacement of bartenders and floor staff. Transfer activity exists in the broader restaurant market, but evidence for control transfers at the assigned firm size is weak.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can consolidate demand forecasting, roster creation, applicant screening, training support, promotion design, guest messaging, purchasing suggestions, and exception detection across venues. Digital ordering and payment can remove transactional steps. Beverage preparation, guest rapport, security, cleaning, crowd management, and responsible service remain difficult to substitute, and current restaurant evidence indicates augmentation is much more common than job elimination.
narrative.value_capture: Posted menu pricing provides room to retain initial labor and administrative savings because there is no contractual cost-plus pass-through. Over time, competitors can share those gains through promotions, better service, or lower effective prices, and operators may reinvest savings in higher-skilled staff. Persistent retention therefore depends on differentiated concepts, local density, disciplined pricing, and measurable service quality.
narrative.firm_availability: Most firms coded to this industry fit the repeat external-customer lens, but qualifying availability is less certain than basic eligibility. The visible restaurant resale market is active yet concentrated in businesses much smaller than the assigned EBITDA band. Liquor licenses, leases, founder identity, and venue-specific goodwill can complicate control transfers or turn them into asset sales.
narrative.demand_durability: The venue experience is hard to replace with software, supporting a high operator-required share. Aggregate quantity is less certain: BLS projects modest drinking-place employment growth, while recent per-capita alcohol consumption declined. Population growth and experiential spending can support visits, but health moderation, at-home consumption, cannabis substitution, and discretionary-spending pressure can offset them.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gaps are bar-specific task time, realized labor savings, lower-middle-market deal incidence, and inflation-adjusted on-premises quantity. Broader restaurant surveys may not transfer to nightclubs, taverns, and cocktail bars. Labor, rent, insurance, security, liquor regulation, lease assignment, and local concept risk can absorb automation gains, while aggressive self-service could damage the hospitality experience that customers are buying.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat transactional demand allows standardized AI-enabled support workflows to be deployed across multiple regulated venues while retaining local hospitality.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most wage-bearing work is physical and customer-facing, and direct evidence on transferable lower-middle-market bar firms is sparse.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 722410 Drinking Places (Alcoholic Beverages) definition
sources.supports: Defines the industry as bars, taverns, nightclubs, and drinking places primarily preparing and serving alcoholic beverages for immediate consumption, with limited food services allowed, and identifies excluded adjacent activities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 722400
sources.supports: Reports 411,940 total employees; food preparation and serving at 83.42% of employment; bartenders at 44.21%; waiters and waitresses at 12.24%; cooks at 11.90%; management at 4.20%; protective service at 4.50%; and occupation-specific mean wages.

sources.title: National Restaurant Association Research Insight: Hiring & Staffing Report 2026
sources.supports: Reports 49% scheduling-software use, 26% AI-tool use, AI impact by workflow, and that 6% of operators said recent technology investment permanently eliminated jobs.

sources.title: National Restaurant Association Restaurant Technology Landscape Report 2024
sources.supports: Reports operator technology plans, including 52% for back-office and inventory systems, 37% for automated labor management, 30% for flexible pricing, and 16% for AI; also reports consumer acceptance of ordering and payment technology alongside a preference for employee engagement in full-service experiences.

sources.title: Restaurant Sales Found Their Footing Late in 2025
sources.supports: Reports 2,516 restaurant transactions in 2025, median revenue of $675,788, median cash flow of $115,252, a median sale price of $215,000, and revenue growth that substantially exceeded cash-flow growth.

sources.title: New Association report provides a demographic profile of restaurant owners
sources.supports: Summarizes Census-based restaurant ownership demographics and reports that 29% of restaurant owners were under age 45.

sources.title: BLS Table 2.11 Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: Projects drinking-place employment from 421,200 in 2024 to 439,900 in 2034, a 0.4% compound annual increase, and broader food-services-and-drinking-places real output growth of 2.2% annually.

sources.title: NIAAA Surveillance Report 122: Apparent Per Capita Alcohol Consumption, 1977 to 2023
sources.supports: Reports 2023 US per-capita ethanol consumption of 2.48 gallons, down 1.2% from 2022, with beer and wine declines offsetting continued spirits growth.

sources.title: TTB Beverage Alcohol Retailers
sources.supports: States that bars and taverns are retail liquor dealers subject to registration and location-specific recordkeeping requirements for beverage alcohol receipts.
